agentic-workflows.meta.description: Build AI-powered automation with GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) — natural language markdown workflows compiled into hardened GitHub Actions.
agentic-workflows.meta.title: Agentic Workflows - DevAI Day Workshop
agentic-workflows.progress.label: Progress
agentic-workflows.sidebar.title: Agentic Workflows
agentic-workflows.step1.automateLi1: Issue triage — Read new issues, ask clarifying questions, apply labels.
agentic-workflows.step1.automateLi2: Code review — Summarize PRs and flag risky changes.
agentic-workflows.step1.automateLi3: Release management — Draft release notes from merged PRs.
agentic-workflows.step1.automateLi4: Maintenance — Keep docs, changelogs, and dependencies tidy.
agentic-workflows.step1.automateP1: Many ready-made examples live in the Agentics collection.
agentic-workflows.step1.comparisonLi1: Understand context — Read your repository, issues, and pull requests to grasp the current situation.
agentic-workflows.step1.comparisonLi2: Make decisions — Choose appropriate actions based on context, not just predefined conditions.
agentic-workflows.step1.comparisonLi3: Adapt behavior — Respond flexibly to scenarios you didn't explicitly program for.
agentic-workflows.step1.comparisonP1: Traditional CI automation follows fixed if-then rules. Agentic workflows use coding agents (like Copilot CLI, Claude, or Codex) that go further:
agentic-workflows.step1.glanceLi1: Frontmatter — A YAML block at the top that configures when the workflow runs and what it is allowed to do (triggers, permissions, safe-outputs).
agentic-workflows.step1.glanceLi2: Markdown body — Your natural-language instructions describing the task.
agentic-workflows.step1.glanceP1: An agentic workflow is a single markdown file with two parts:
agentic-workflows.step1.glanceP2: The gh aw compile command converts this markdown into a hardened .lock.yml GitHub Actions workflow that runs an AI agent in a containerized environment whenever the trigger fires.
agentic-workflows.step1.glanceTitle: How It Works at a Glance
agentic-workflows.step1.intro: GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) are AI-powered automation that can understand context, make decisions, and take meaningful actions — all from natural-language instructions you write in markdown. Instead of brittle scripts, you describe what you want to happen and let a coding agent figure out how.
agentic-workflows.step1.keyIdeaP1: A traditional workflow is code that runs. An agentic workflow is natural language that an AI agent runs — compiled into a hardened GitHub Actions workflow so it executes safely and repeatably.
agentic-workflows.step1.title: What are Agentic Workflows?
agentic-workflows.step2.anatomyP1: Workflows live as markdown files under .github/workflows/. Here is a minimal example:
agentic-workflows.step2.anatomyP2: The YAML frontmatter (between the --- markers) controls execution; the markdown body is the agent's task in plain language.
agentic-workflows.step2.challengeP1: Look at the minimal example above. In one sentence each, answer: when does this workflow run, what can it read, and what is the single thing it's allowed to write back? Being able to answer these three at a glance is the core safety skill for gh-aw.
agentic-workflows.step2.challengeTitle: Mini Challenge (2 min): Read the frontmatter
agentic-workflows.step2.initP1: gh aw init prepares the repository for agentic workflows — it configures .gitattributes and scaffolds supporting skill/agent files.
agentic-workflows.step2.intro: The gh aw CLI extension lets you create, compile, and run agentic workflows directly from your terminal.
agentic-workflows.step2.prereqLi1: GitHub CLI (gh) installed and authenticated — cli.github.com
agentic-workflows.step2.prereqLi2: A repository with GitHub Actions enabled and permission to add workflows
agentic-workflows.step2.prereqLi3: A coding agent / model available to your repo (e.g., GitHub Copilot)
agentic-workflows.step2.tipP1: You can start from a curated example instead of a blank file with gh aw add, which pulls in a workflow from the Agentics collection and generates its compiled YAML.
agentic-workflows.step2.title: Installing gh-aw & Workflow Anatomy
agentic-workflows.step3.challengeP1: Imagine an agent that only needs to label issues. Write the safe-outputs block that grants the least privilege required — just add-labels with an allowed list — and nothing more. Then ask yourself: what's the worst a compromised prompt could do with this configuration?
agentic-workflows.step3.challengeTitle: Mini Challenge (3 min): Tighten the permissions
agentic-workflows.step3.intro: Giving an AI agent access to your repository demands a strong security model. The frontmatter is where you keep agents on a short, well-defined leash.
agentic-workflows.step3.leastPrivilegeP1: Declare only the safe-outputs a workflow actually needs, and constrain them (e.g., an allowed label list). Access can also be gated to team members so untrusted contributors can't trigger privileged agent actions.
agentic-workflows.step3.permissionsP1: Agentic workflows default to read-only permissions. The agent can read context but cannot directly write to your repository.
agentic-workflows.step3.safeOutputsP1: Because the agent has no direct write access, all write operations flow through safe-outputs: a set of sanitized, validated GitHub operations. The agent proposes an output; gh-aw validates and performs it.
agentic-workflows.step3.safeOutputsP2: This separation means even if the agent is led astray, it can only perform the narrow, pre-approved actions you declared — never arbitrary writes.
agentic-workflows.step3.title: Triggers, Permissions & Safe-Outputs
agentic-workflows.step3.triggersP1: The on: block defines when a workflow runs, using the same event syntax as GitHub Actions.
agentic-workflows.step3.whyLi1: No direct write access — Reduces blast radius if an agent misbehaves.
agentic-workflows.step3.whyLi2: Validated operations — Outputs are sanitized before they touch your repo.
agentic-workflows.step3.whyLi3: Auditable — Each action is explicit in the frontmatter and visible in run logs.
agentic-workflows.step4.challengeP1: The .md file is what you edit, but the committed .lock.yml is what actually runs. In your own words, why does gh-aw compile to a locked YAML file instead of running the markdown directly? (Hint: think about reproducibility and reviewing exactly what will execute.)
agentic-workflows.step4.compileLi3: Embeds the frontmatter and loads the markdown body at runtime.
agentic-workflows.step4.compileP1: For each name.md, gh-aw generates a name.lock.yml — the compiled workflow GitHub Actions actually runs. Compilation:
agentic-workflows.step4.intro: A markdown workflow doesn't run directly — it is compiled into a hardened GitHub Actions workflow first. Think of it like compiling code: you write human-friendly markdown, the compiler produces machine-ready YAML.
agentic-workflows.step4.lockP1: Commit both the .md source and the generated .lock.yml. The lock file is what runs in Actions; keeping it in version control makes runs reviewable and reproducible. Recompile whenever you change the markdown.
agentic-workflows.step4.runP1: Once committed, the workflow triggers automatically on its configured events. You can also dispatch and inspect runs from the CLI:
agentic-workflows.step4.runP2: Each run spins up an AI agent in a containerized environment, which reads the repository context, performs the task described in your markdown, and emits any declared safe-outputs.
agentic-workflows.step4.title: Compiling & Running
agentic-workflows.step5.checklistP1: For whichever you pick: (1) start from permissions: read-all, (2) allow only the safe-outputs you actually need, (3) keep the prompt focused on one job, (4) compile with gh aw compile, and (5) test against a deliberately flawed example so you can see it fire.
agentic-workflows.step5.chooseP1: You've built the issue-triage agent step by step. Now pick one of the use-cases below and build it yourself using the same pattern: a trigger, read-only permissions, a narrow set of safe-outputs, and a clear natural-language prompt. Each one is a realistic agent teams actually run.
agentic-workflows.step5.extendP1: Add a weekly schedule: trigger that summarizes stale issues, or a PR-review workflow that flags risky diffs. Browse the Agentics collection for inspiration and copy a workflow with gh aw add.
agentic-workflows.step5.goalP1: When an issue is opened, the agent should read it, decide whether it has enough information, comment asking for anything missing, and apply one label from an allowed set.
agentic-workflows.step5.intro: Put it all together by building an agentic workflow that triages newly opened issues: it asks for missing details and applies an appropriate label.
agentic-workflows.step5.nextLi1: Combine this path with Spec-Driven Development — have an agentic workflow turn specs into draft PRs.
agentic-workflows.step5.nextLi2: Tighten security: gate triggers to team members and minimize safe-outputs.
agentic-workflows.step5.nextLi3: Roll an agentic workflow into a real repository and measure the time it saves.
agentic-workflows.step5.stepsLi1: Prepare a test repository with Actions enabled, then run gh aw init.
agentic-workflows.step5.stepsLi3: Compile it with gh aw compile and commit both files.
agentic-workflows.step5.stepsLi4: Open a deliberately vague test issue and watch the agent respond.
agentic-workflows.step5.title: Hands-on: Build an Issue-Triage Agent
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase1Goal: Goal: Catch docs that fall out of sync with the code they describe.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase1Li1: Trigger: pull_request (on changes to source files), or a weekly schedule:.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase1Li2: What it does: Compares changed code against related docs/README sections and flags mismatches (renamed functions, changed flags, removed options).
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase1Li3: Safe-outputs: add-comment on the PR listing the drift, or create-issue for scheduled scans.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase1Li4: Stretch: Have it propose the corrected doc snippet inline.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase2Goal: Goal: Nudge tests toward a clear Arrange–Act–Assert structure.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase2Li2: What it does: Reviews added/modified tests and flags ones with no clear arrange/act/assert separation, multiple assertions testing unrelated things, or missing edge cases.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase2Li4: Stretch: Apply a needs-test-cleanup label when issues are found.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase3Goal: Goal: Surface likely-exploitable issues early, with low noise.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase3Li2: What it does: Looks for hard-coded secrets, unvalidated input reaching sensitive sinks, missing authz checks, and unsafe deserialization — reporting only high-confidence findings.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase3Li4: Stretch: Require a maintainer ack before the PR can merge.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase4Goal: Goal: Spot copy-paste and near-duplicate logic worth refactoring.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase4Li1: Trigger: pull_request, or a weekly schedule: across the repo.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase4Li2: What it does: Identifies blocks that are structurally similar to existing code and suggests where a shared helper could live.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase4Li3: Safe-outputs: add-comment pointing at the duplicate locations, or create-issue with a refactor proposal.
agentic-workflows.step5.useCase4Li4: Stretch: Have it draft the extracted helper function in the comment.
agentic-workflows.step5.verifyLi1: A vague issue receives a polite comment requesting specifics and a needs-info label.
agentic-workflows.step5.verifyLi2: A well-formed bug report receives a bug label and no unnecessary comment.
agentic-workflows.step5.verifyLi3: The run logs (gh aw logs) show the agent's reasoning and the safe-outputs it emitted.
